strengths: The buyer/trigger/offering/pricing chain is now internally coherent end-to-end, which it was not in v4. v5's central fix is correct and load-bearing: it re-picked the trigger (reviewer-capacity strain / cheaper-than-the-next-hire) to match the buyer it deliberately chose (the 50-150-person, has-a-controller, multi-system firm). The pricing then anchors against the right comparator -- the fully-loaded cost of a $110-160k senior accountant hire -- so a $30-38k First Close plus a $5-9k/mo retainer reads as 'a fraction of an FTE,' which is a defensible value frame, not a wishful one. This is the single hardest coherence test a solo-services concept must pass and v5 passes it.

The honesty about scale is now an asset rather than a wound. v5 stops claiming software leverage it does not have, names the moat precisely (one buildable asset = integrated cross-system lineage + governance trail; two non-transferable = Matt's judgment and founder-fit), and reframes the reconciliation work as bespoke-per-customer with a compounding pattern library that speeds hand-assembly but never makes the next customer automatic. That is the correct technical and commercial read and it kills the four most dangerous v4 fantasies (general engine, circular floor math, free-but-counted subscription, deal-at-risk trigger).

Year 1 is now correctly modeled as a funded runway, not an income year, with the $160-200k floor moved to Year 2 where it belongs. The design-partner phase is booked as subsidized R&D + customer acquisition rather than dressed up as 'at-cost' revenue. This is the kind of accounting honesty that makes the plan actually executable by a solo operator with a savings/partial-income bridge.

The liability posture (the buyer's own qualified reviewer signs every number; never Matt, never a supplied CPA; numbers come from fixed reviewer-re-run scripts, not LLM free-text) is now the spine of the whole concept and leads the pitch. This is the correct sequencing -- the single-signer rule is what makes everything downstream sellable -- and it resolves the CPA-credential trap that sank v2 and v3.

gaps.title: v5 unilaterally answers Matt's explicit ambition question with the most conservative option and never builds the ambitious variant he asked for -- the brief is half-unaddressed.
gaps.why: Matt's brief states 'NO constraints on ambition (partners/funding/team allowed if value justifies)' and the orchestration explicitly asks every seat to design the credible ambitious variant. v5 instead hard-codes 'solo-forever, ceiling = ~40 delivery weeks, default expectation: stay a consulting service permanently.' That is the critic loop's conclusion, not Matt's. The concept does not contain the one thing the owner asked for: a Track B that survives v5's own critiques. A concept doc that resolves the founder's stated central question by silently overruling it is unexecutable as the answer to the actual brief -- it answers a different, narrower question than the one posed. The honesty is real, but honesty about a solo ceiling is not the same as having tested whether the ceiling is removable.

gaps.title: The bottleneck that caps the business (Matt's senior judgment in delivery) is exactly the function a second delivery person could absorb -- and v5 never tests whether an apprenticeship/second-reviewer-builder model breaks any of its hard-won critiques.
gaps.why: v5 names two non-transferable assets: founder credibility (sells) and senior-judgment quality (delivers). Critically, the binding constraint is delivery weeks, not sales weeks. A second person trained on the pattern library to do the bespoke script-build + mapping under Matt's review attacks the actual ceiling without touching the liability posture (the buyer's reviewer still signs -- the second person is a builder, not a signer, so no CPA-credential problem is introduced). v5 dismisses team as a violation of 'solo' without testing it against its own four critiques. It survives all four: it is not a general engine (still bespoke), it does not require steady-state math in Year 1 (Year-2+ move), it does not resurrect the subscription, and it does not change the trigger. The doc forecloses the highest-leverage ambition lever without analysis.

gaps.title: The governance IP (tribunal + posture + Saga log) is treated only as an internal delivery trust-mechanism, but the research synthesis ranked it the #1 differentiable, defensible white space (S1) -- and Verdict was the highest-conviction original candidate. v5 de-grafts it to a 'gated, low-probability fork' with no funded path, which may be discarding the most valuable asset.
gaps.why: The synthesis is unambiguous: S1 says the binding in-loop tribunal is genuine white space no competitor has built; T1 was ranked the top territory; the recommended posture was 'lead commercially with the finance deliverable, productize the governance IP as the IP that the consulting sells.' v5 honors the first half and abandons the second -- the governance product is now contingent on a stability sample the 18-month plan 'barely reaches,' with 'stay consulting' as the default. That collapses a two-track strategy (consulting funds + proves the IP; IP becomes a separable product) into a one-track service. If the governance IP is genuinely the rare asset, mothballing it as low-probability upside is a strategic under-allocation, not honesty. The finance wedge is the proving ground the synthesis designed FOR the IP; v5 keeps the proving ground and quietly cancels the thing being proved.

gaps.title: No second income/leverage line survives anywhere in the model, and v5 treats that as settled fact rather than a solvable design problem -- the Aegis/Leash evidence-pack-as-product idea was killed for the wrong reason.
gaps.why: v4's subscription was correctly killed because it had no standalone payers *among the finance-retainer base*. But the original Aegis and Leash candidates targeted a DIFFERENT buyer (the insurance-renewal / vendor-questionnaire owner at a 5-200 person shop) with a different trigger that the synthesis (S3, the single most surprising finding) rated as biting *right now* on an annual clock. v5 folded the evidence pack into the finance retainer and inherited the finance buyer's emptiness, but never asked whether the evidence pack stands alone for the original insurance-triggered buyer. That may be a real, separable, lower-touch line that the finance concept simply doesn't serve -- discarded by conflation, not by analysis.

closures: Add an explicit Track B section to the concept titled 'The ambitious variant -- and the gates that unlock it.' Keep v5's solo plan as Track A (the default, fully honest). Track B is NOT a different business; it is the same Closebook + Leash concept with three sequenced, gated ambition levers, each of which must survive v5's four critiques. State plainly: Track A is what runs by default; Track B is what Matt deliberately triggers if a named gate is met. This directly answers Matt's 'no constraints on ambition' instruction while preserving every hard-won honesty fix -- the two are not in tension when the ambition is gated rather than assumed.

Track B, Lever 1 (the calendar-ceiling lever -- a second delivery person, NOT a second signer): Once Year 2 steady-state is reached and the pattern library demonstrably cuts build hours (the doc already instruments this), bring on an apprentice analyst/builder who does mapping + bespoke script-build under Matt's review. Liability posture is untouched: the buyer's reviewer still signs; the apprentice is a builder, not an attestor; Matt remains the senior reviewer of the build, not the signer of the number. Gate: >=4 full-price closes delivered AND measured build-hour reduction AND a 6-month cash buffer. This roughly doubles delivery capacity (the actual binding constraint) without introducing any CPA-credential dependency or general-engine bet. Survives all four v4 critiques.

Track B, Lever 2 (the sales-leverage lever -- a referral/origination partner who sells while Matt delivers): v5's own Risk #6 already names fractional-CFO / outsourced-controller firms as the warm referral channel. Formalize one as a revenue-share origination partner so selling stops competing with Matt's scarce delivery weeks. This is the lowest-cost ambition lever (no headcount on Matt's books, no liability change) and should be tested first, in Year 1, because it also de-risks the single-channel concentration the critic flagged.

Track B, Lever 3 (the IP-productization lever -- fund the governance IP on a separate, gated track, aligned to the synthesis's S1/T1): Re-graft the Verdict / Leash governance product as an explicit Track B option with a real (not merely 'someday') decision gate: IF the finance engagements prove >=3 recurring finance-judgment decision categories that route cleanly through thing-decide.py AND a design partner's reviewer confirms the lineage-card-with-verdict is decision-useful, THEN scope the governance gateway as a separable product for the developer/compliance buyer -- the buyer the synthesis identified, distinct from the finance buyer. Fund it from consulting cash or a small raise explicitly earmarked for IP, NOT from Matt's delivery weeks. This is where 'funding allowed if value justifies' (Matt's words) actually applies: the IP, not the service, is the fundable thing.

Re-graft the insurance/vendor-questionnaire evidence-pack as a candidate standalone Track B micro-line aimed at its ORIGINAL buyer (the 5-200-person owner facing a renewal/procurement deadline), not the finance retainer base. Add one validation gate before counting any revenue: re-verify the ISO GenAI E&O exclusion + carrier-language trigger against the primary filing (the doc already flags this as unverified). If the trigger verifies AND the evidence pack can regenerate from disk with no live tribunal cost (already true per Risk #10), this is the one genuinely lower-touch, non-calendar-bound line in the whole portfolio -- exactly the scale lever v5 says is absent. Keep it gated; do not count it until the trigger verifies.

Add a one-paragraph 'Why we are NOT discarding the original candidates' note that explicitly records the disposition of each of the five original ideas, so the de-scoping is a decision, not a drift: Verdict -> Track B Lever 3; Aegis/Leash evidence pack -> Track B insurance micro-line (gated on trigger verification); Forge -> retained as internal delivery method (correct, unchanged); Closebook -> the Track A core. This makes the regrafting auditable and prevents a future session from re-discovering an idea v5 silently dropped.
enhancements: Reframe the doc's mental model from 'is v5 too small?' to 'v5 is correctly sized as the DEFAULT, and the ambition is a set of named, gated levers stacked on top.' This is the cleanest reconciliation of the brief's two constraints (no ambition ceiling + simple to understand): the business stays simple and honest by default, and ambition is opt-in via gates rather than baked into the base-case math. It lets Matt say yes to ambition without re-litigating the runway honesty.

Make the pattern library's measured build-hour reduction the master gate for ALL of Track B. Every ambition lever (second builder, partner, IP product) is only safe to pull once the compounding claim is proven with numbers, which v5 already commits to instrumenting from day one. Tying the ambition gates to the metric v5 already collects means no new measurement apparatus is needed -- the trigger for 'go ambitious' is the same data the base plan already produces.

Sequence the three ambition levers by cost-to-test, lowest first: (1) origination partner (Year 1, near-zero cost, also de-risks the sourcing concentration); (2) second delivery person (Year 2, after build-hour reduction is proven); (3) IP productization / possible raise (Year 2+, the most capital- and focus-intensive, gated hardest). This ordering means Matt learns whether ambition pays before committing scarce capital or headcount -- the ambitious variant becomes a series of cheap call-options, not a bet-the-runway pivot.

For the IP track specifically, note that funding (Matt's explicitly-permitted lever) is better matched to the governance IP than to the consulting service: a services business funded by a raise just buys Matt's calendar back, which the pattern library already does more cheaply; but the governance product (S1 white space, ~12-18 month window per the synthesis's C4) is a genuine race against incumbents where capital buys speed-to-market that compounds. If Matt ever takes outside money, this is the line item it should fund -- a precise, defensible answer to 'funding allowed if value justifies.'

seat: Skeptical Buyer / CFO — controller at an 80-person multi-system company who also approves the spend
strengths: The trigger reframe from 'deal-at-risk deadline' to 'reviewer-capacity strain / cheaper than the next hire' is exactly right and is the single most buyer-true correction in the document. As the controller I do clear the diligence deadline every time — that IS my job — so a vitamin-priced-as-painkiller pitch would have gotten me off the phone. 'Your existing reviewer is maxed and the company keeps asking for more' is the conversation that actually happens in my 1:1 with the CFO. v5 finally sells to a pain I'd put in a budget request.

The single-signer rule (my own in-house reviewer signs, never Matt, never a supplied CPA) is the thing that lets me even take the meeting. The moment a vendor offers to 'sign' or supply a CPA to attest numbers they didn't compute, I hang up — I've seen that liability blow up. v5 puts the liability posture in the headline instead of the footnotes, which is the correct ordering for a finance buyer.

Section 3b ('why beats Excel Copilot / our accounting suite's native AI') correctly identifies my real first objection. I already have in-tool AI drafting. The cross-system stitch + a third-party-facing signed package + a governance trail the auditor asks about is a genuine distinction my in-tool assistant does NOT do, and naming it pre-empts the 'why wouldn't I just use what I have' that kills most of these pitches.

The honesty about the demo — explicitly conceding a canned sample proves output-shape-and-control-surface, not that you've reconciled MY books — is the kind of candor that actually raises my trust. Vendors who pretend the polished demo equals my data lose me; vendors who say 'here's what the first working session with your exports really costs in time' get a second meeting.

Naming the fractional-CFO / outsourced-controller firm + my own audit/lender relationships as a referral channel is correct: those firms sit next to exactly my profile, refer rather than compete, and a warm intro from my auditor is worth more than any cold outreach. As a buyer, that's also how I'd actually first hear about this.

gaps.title: The economic buyer is not the bottleneck — the controller feels the pain but does NOT hold the $30-38k discretionary budget; v5 never identifies who actually signs the PO or how the controller gets it approved
gaps.why: At an 80-person company the controller (the maxed-out person v5 targets) almost never owns a $30-38k+ouf-of-budget discretionary spend — the CFO, VP Finance, or owner does. v5 conflates 'who feels the pain' with 'who approves the spend' and addresses only the former. The whole sale stalls at the moment the controller has to walk into the CFO's office and justify $30-38k + $5-9k/mo against either (a) a permanent hire the CFO can headcount-plan, or (b) 'just work the existing tools harder.' The doc gives the controller no ammunition for that internal sale: no ROI framing in the approver's language, no 'here is the one-page business case your CFO will sign.' A pitch that wins the user but has no path to the approver dies in procurement — and I am BOTH people, so I see both halves fail to connect.

gaps.title: The 'cheaper than a $110-160k hire' comparison is asserted but never computed at the buyer's decision table — and on the buyer's real math it often LOSES to the hire
gaps.why: v5 prices the engagement at $30-38k + a $5-9k/mo retainer. The retainer alone is $60-108k/yr. Add the First Close and Year-1 all-in to me is ~$90-146k — i.e. inside the same band as the $110-160k fully-loaded senior accountant v5 says it beats, except the hire is permanent owned capacity, builds institutional knowledge, can flex to other work, and isn't a single-person-dependency on Matt's calendar. v5 never lays out the buyer-side TCO comparison it leans on, so the central value claim ('fraction of the cost') is not demonstrated where I'd actually evaluate it. As the CFO I run that math in 90 seconds and the engagement does not obviously win — it wins only if the retainer is far cheaper or far lower-touch than stated, which v5's own 'highest-liability, highest-touch line' framing contradicts.

gaps.title: Time-to-first-value is still unbounded for exactly my profile — an 80-person MULTI-system shop is the 'unusual stack' v5 quietly excludes from its 'week or two' promise
gaps.why: v5 honestly retired the 5-minute claim, but the replacement — 'actual first close on screen within a week or two FOR A COMMON SYSTEM STACK, longer if your stack is unusual' — has a load-bearing escape hatch. The buyers most worth $30-38k (multi-system, has-a-controller, capacity-strained) are precisely the ones with the messiest, least-common stacks — that's WHY their close is hard. So the buyers who feel the pain hardest are systematically the ones for whom the time-to-value is 'longer, hand-built, unbounded.' On a sales call I will ask 'how long for MY stack' and the honest answer is 'we don't know until we map it.' That's an unpriced, open-ended commitment of MY controller's time during a 4-6 week design-partner phase — exactly when she's already the bottleneck. The cure (relieve the bottleneck) requires spending the bottleneck's scarcest resource up front, and v5 doesn't quantify or de-risk that.

gaps.title: The retainer — the only recurring revenue and the thing that makes the unit economics work — is priced as the highest-touch line, which means it competes directly with the hire on an ongoing basis and never gets cheap enough to be a clear win
gaps.why: v5 candidly admits the run-it retainer 'does not get low-touch until' per-customer scripts mature 'after several months on that account.' So for the first many months the buyer pays $5-9k/mo for a high-touch service whose throughput gain is real but whose cost is within shouting distance of amortized headcount — and unlike headcount, it's a single-person dependency. The retainer is simultaneously (a) the lever that clears the Year-2 floor and (b) the line a cost-conscious CFO is most likely to cut first when budgets tighten, precisely because it reads as 'an ongoing consultant fee' rather than 'an asset we own.' v5 doesn't model retainer churn risk from the buyer's seat or give the buyer a reason the retainer is stickier than a contractor they'd drop in a downturn.

gaps.title: 'Bespoke per-customer scripts the buyer's reviewer re-runs and signs' creates a key-person / handoff dependency that I, as the buyer, must price as a risk — what happens to my monthly close if Matt is unavailable?
gaps.why: v5 is admirably honest that the deliverable is bespoke per-customer code hand-built by Matt-plus-agent, and that 'senior-judgment quality leaves when he's at capacity or stops.' From the buyer's chair that is a continuity risk on my CORE monthly close: if my signed close now depends on customer-specific scripts only Matt fully understands, and Matt is one person with a 40-week ceiling, I've introduced a single point of failure into my most deadline-critical process. v5 names this as a moat-honesty point but never addresses it as a buyer-facing concern with a mitigation (escrow of the scripts, documentation handoff, a 'you own and can re-run the code without us' clause). A conservative finance buyer will not put their close on a one-person dependency without that assurance.

closures: Add a short 'Section 3c — the internal sale: arming the bottleneck to get budget' that explicitly separates the user (controller, feels pain) from the approver (CFO/owner, holds budget) and ships a one-page business-case template in the approver's language: hours-saved x loaded controller rate, avoided-hire cost, faster-close value to the board, and a clear ask. State plainly that the first sales motion targets the approver with the controller as the internal champion — not the controller alone. This converts a P0 dead-end into a two-stakeholder sale the doc actually plans for.

Insert a buyer-side TCO comparison table directly opposite the hire: Year-1 all-in (First Close + N months retainer) vs. a $110-160k fully-loaded senior accountant, AND vs. 'do nothing / work the tools harder.' Show the honest cases where the engagement wins (capacity needed for <12 months, no headcount available, board wants faster close NOW) and concede the cases where a hire wins (permanent need, wants owned capacity). A CFO trusts the vendor who shows them when NOT to buy; this also forces v5 to prove its own central claim rather than assert it.

Replace the open-ended 'longer if your stack is unusual' with a bounded, productized discovery step: a fixed-price, fixed-duration 'Mapping Assessment' (e.g. $3-5k, 1 week) that ends in a written estimate of time-to-first-close for THAT buyer's specific stack before they commit to the full engagement. This caps the buyer's open-ended time/cost exposure, gives the controller a small low-risk first 'yes,' and lets Matt qualify out genuinely unbuildable stacks before sinking design-partner weeks into them.

Reframe the retainer around buyer-owned assets, not ongoing labor: bundle into the retainer a quarterly handover of the documented, re-runnable scripts the buyer's reviewer already operates, so the buyer is paying for maintenance + exception-handling of an asset they own and could run without Matt — not for indefinite dependence. Price a visible step-down (high-touch first 3-6 months, lower-touch 'exceptions-only' rate once scripts mature) so the buyer sees the cost curve bending down, which both justifies the premium early and reduces churn-on-budget-cut later.

Add a buyer-facing continuity clause and name it in Section 9 Risk #5: the customer receives, owns, and can independently re-run all bespoke reconciliation/close scripts (plain-language operating brief + the code), so the monthly close survives Matt's unavailability. Make 'you are never locked to us to run your own close' an explicit selling point — it turns the honest single-person-dependency admission into a trust asset rather than a buyer objection.
enhancements: On the 'is v5 over-corrected / what would the AMBITIOUS variant be' question Matt explicitly raised: the critic loop correctly killed the SOLO-builds-a-general-engine fantasy, but it over-rotated into 'solo forever, hard 40-week ceiling.' The ambitious variant that still SURVIVES every v5 critique is not 'build BlackLine' — it's adding a second human, specifically a fractional/contract CPA-or-senior-controller as a delivery PARTNER (not a signer of others' work). This breaks the two single-person dependencies v5 itself names as the binding ceiling (judgment quality + founder credibility now have a second carrier), roughly doubles the delivery-week ceiling, de-risks the key-person continuity gap above, and keeps the liability posture intact because the partner signs nothing they didn't deliver. It does NOT require the un-buildable general engine or VC funding. This is the highest-leverage ambition move that doesn't reintroduce a killed risk — v5 should at least name it as the Year-2/3 scale fork instead of defaulting to 'solo permanently.'

A genuinely productizable asset hides inside v5 that it under-sells: the EVIDENCE PACK / lineage trail is buyer-valuable independent of who built the numbers, and it's the one thing in the catalog that maps onto a recurring external forcing function (auditor / lender / insurer questionnaire) rather than internal pain. Rather than demoting it to a free retainer feature, the ambitious-but-survivable move is to spin the evidence-pack + governance-trail as a SEPARATE, lighter, self-serve-ish product sold to a DIFFERENT and larger buyer (any finance team that already has clean numbers but needs to show AI-controls evidence at renewal) — funded by the consulting cash, gated on the same '3 engagements prove the categories' test. This is the only line in the whole concept with software-margin potential, and v5 just deleted it; it should instead be the explicitly-gated scale bet.

Add a 'land small, prove value, expand' on-ramp the current pricing lacks: the $30-38k First Close is a big first commitment for a buyer who hasn't seen value on their own books. Pair the bounded Mapping Assessment (closure #3) with a single-deliverable entry product — e.g. 'one clean, fully-sourced 13-week cash forecast for $8-12k' — that delivers a real artifact on the buyer's actual data in 2 weeks. It de-risks the buyer's first dollar, gives Matt a faster reference-customer cycle, and is the kind of small concrete win a controller can get approved without a full CFO budget fight. The full First Close becomes the expand, not the land.

Instrument and PUBLISH the time-savings claim as the sales asset it should be: v5 already plans to instrument delivery hours internally. Turn that outward — every engagement should produce an anonymized 'before: controller spent N days; after: M hours' metric, accumulated into a credibility table. For a skeptical finance buyer, two or three real before/after numbers from comparable companies beat every paragraph of positioning in the doc, and directly answer the 'cheaper than a hire' math the buyer runs.

strengths: Buyer-trigger realignment is now commercially coherent: v5 sells against the cost of the NEXT FINANCE HIRE ($110-160k loaded senior accountant) for a has-a-reviewer, capacity-strained 50-150-person company. That is a real, budgeted, recurring comparison line with a named alternative — the single hardest thing to get right in a GTM motion, and v5 got it right. The price band ($30-38k First Close + retainer) reads as 'a fraction of an FTE,' which is exactly the frame that converts.

Founder-fit is a genuine, hard-to-copy GTM asset, not a slide. Matt IS a financial analyst selling to finance operators — native-habitat credibility means warm intros, shared vocabulary, and a demo that survives a skeptical controller's questions. v5 correctly names this as non-transferable (it's Matt) rather than dressing it as a moat, which is the honest framing a sophisticated buyer respects.

The competitive-window framing against Excel Copilot / accounting-suite AI is sharp and defensible: the wedge is cross-system assembly + a third-party-facing signed package + a governance trail — three jobs an in-tool drafting assistant structurally does not do. Section 3b articulates this better than most funded competitors articulate their own differentiation.

The 'invisible machinery, visible evidence' packaging rule (Section 6) is the correct resolution of the synthesis's C2 contradiction and is GTM-load-bearing: the buyer never sees 'tribunal,' 'YAML,' or 'governance' — only 'what our AI is allowed to do / what we blocked / how every number traces back.' This keeps the pitch legible to a non-technical finance buyer while preserving the trust signal.

The fractional-CFO / outsourced-controller referral channel (Risk #6) is a genuinely good second channel: those firms sit adjacent to the exact has-a-reviewer, capacity-strained buyer, refer rather than compete (they don't sell AI tooling), and convert warm. This is the strongest non-obvious GTM move in the document.

gaps.title: The name 'Closebook + Leash' is a positioning liability, not an asset, for the buyer v5 actually chose.
gaps.why: v5 narrowed the buyer to a company that ALREADY has a controller signing the close. To that buyer, 'Leash' (a fear/control metaphor borrowed from the dev-governance candidate ideas) answers a question they haven't asked yet — it foregrounds 'restrain the dangerous AI,' which Section 6 itself concedes is 'a fear the buyer doesn't yet have.' Worse, a two-noun compound name forces the prospect to hold two concepts at once, violating Matt's own 'simple to understand' constraint and the document's own lead-with-the-deliverable order. 'Closebook' is fine and finance-legible; 'Leash' is the governance-product residue from a different buyer (the dev/insurance Leash candidate, idea #5) and it drags the pitch toward the quieter-governance posture v5 elsewhere correctly adopts. A name has to do the positioning work in three seconds at a referral intro; 'Closebook + Leash' does not.

gaps.title: No stated time-to-first-dollar or pipeline-coverage math — the channel-realism question is asserted, not modeled.
gaps.why: v5 names two channels (Matt's finance network; fractional-CFO referrals) but never models whether they produce enough qualified pipeline to land 1-2 design partners in Months 0-3 and 2-4 full-price by Month 18. Finance is a slow, conservative, reference-driven sale (the document admits this in Risk #5 of the candidate and in the trust-friction risks). For a solo operator with no reference customer in Year 1, a realistic close rate on warm finance intros is low single-digit per quarter, and the sales cycle on a $30-38k engagement to a risk-averse controller is plausibly 2-4 months. The roadmap's '1-2 design partners by Month 3' is a pipeline claim with no coverage ratio behind it. If it takes 8-12 qualified conversations to land one design partner and Matt's warm network yields ~15-20 finance-operator contacts, the math is tight and unstated — and a stalled top-of-funnel is the most likely failure mode of the entire plan, more likely than the build risk.

gaps.title: The demo motion concedes its own weakness without fully closing it — the 'run it on MY books' objection still kills momentum.
gaps.why: Section 6 is admirably honest that a canned sample demo proves output-shape + leash, not that we've reconciled the prospect's books, and that the real proof takes a paid mapping working-session. But honesty about the gap is not the same as a motion that converts across it. As written, the skeptical controller watches a polished sample, hears 'the real thing takes a paid hours-to-days session,' and has no de-risked, low-commitment next step — the jump from free demo to a $30-38k engagement (or even an $18-22k design-partner) is a cliff. There is no paid diagnostic / paid pilot rung on the ladder that lets a cautious buyer buy a small, bounded proof on their OWN data before committing to the full engagement. Without that rung, the demo's honesty becomes a conversion leak.

gaps.title: The governance/Leash story is currently pitched at the wrong volume AND the wrong sequence for a finance buyer.
gaps.why: v5 keeps the 'ask the AI to do something reckless and watch the Stop block it' as the trust-close of the demo. For the has-a-reviewer finance buyer, this is a solution to a problem they don't yet feel — and Risk #4 admits the headline governance asset (gated-AI-verdict under a number) isn't even wired to finance yet. Leading or even co-leading with the Stop spends credibility on an aspirational capability. The governance story's RIGHT job in this buyer's pitch is narrow and quiet: it answers the auditor's/lender's 'how was this number built and who controlled the AI' question — i.e., it's a feature of the lineage card (defensibility), not a standalone act in the demo. The document half-knows this (it orders deliverable-first) but still stages the Stop as a distinct trust-close, which re-inflates the volume it just turned down.

closures: Drop 'Leash' from the buyer-facing name. Lead with 'Closebook' alone as the product/service name, and describe the governance layer in-line as a feature ('every number carries a verifiable source-and-control trail') rather than as a co-equal named thing. Reserve 'Leash' strictly for the internal/dev-governance vocabulary and the gated Phase-2 fork (where the buyer IS a developer and the metaphor lands). Test two finance-legible taglines at the first three referral intros: 'Closebook — your controller's close and 13-week cash, drafted and fully sourced, signed in hours not days' vs. 'Closebook — get more close out of the controller you already have.' Pick by which one earns the second meeting.

Add a one-paragraph pipeline-coverage model to Section 7 or 8: state the assumed warm-network size, the assumed qualified-conversation-to-design-partner ratio, and the implied number of intros needed to hit '1-2 design partners by Month 3' and '2-4 full-price by Month 18.' Then make the fractional-CFO referral channel a Month-0 activity, not a Risk-6 contingency — court 2-3 fractional-CFO/outsourced-controller practices BEFORE the personal network is exhausted, because referral channels have their own lead time (you have to earn the referrer's trust first). If the coverage math doesn't close, that is the signal to widen the buyer definition or lengthen the runway, surfaced as a precondition rather than discovered mid-Year-1.

Insert a paid-diagnostic rung between the free sample demo and the First Close engagement: a fixed-fee (~$3-5k), tightly-bounded 'Close Readiness + First-Number' session on the prospect's OWN exports — Matt + the agent reconcile ONE real cross-system tie-out (e.g., billing-to-GL revenue) and produce one signed-able figure with its lineage card, on their data, in a defined window. This converts the demo's honesty into a low-risk yes, gives the cautious controller a small bounded proof on real books, and the fee credits toward the full engagement if they proceed. It also de-risks Matt's side (he learns the books' messiness before quoting fixed-price). Name it explicitly in the demo motion as the next step.

Re-stage the governance story in the demo: fold the Stop into the lineage-card walkthrough as the answer to a buyer-or-auditor question ('here's how we keep this number safe and prove who controlled the AI that touched it'), shown ON a real figure, rather than as a separate reckless-action theater piece. Until the finance-judgment-to-Saga wiring ships (Risk #4), pitch the governance layer ONLY as the human-authored source trail (real today) plus the Stop-as-safety-feature, and explicitly do not stage the gated-AI-verdict as live. Keep the governance volume at 'quiet feature that earns the auditor's nod,' not 'co-headline.'
enhancements: Sharpest one-line positioning (my recommended lead): 'Closebook gets more close out of the controller you already have — a fully-sourced, cross-system close and 13-week cash draft your reviewer signs in hours, for a fraction of the next finance hire.' It names the buyer (has-a-controller), the trigger (capacity strain), the comparison (the next hire), the deliverable (close + cash), and the proof posture (fully-sourced, reviewer-signed) in one breath — and it omits 'governance,' 'AI,' 'Leash,' and 'tribunal' entirely, which is correct for this buyer.

Build a single anonymized reference artifact into a self-running, click-through web demo (the data-platform embedded-dashboard surface already exists) the moment the first design partner closes. For a reference-driven finance sale, a real (anonymized) lineage-carrying close package that a prospect can click through unsupervised is worth more than any live demo and shortens the trust cycle materially. Make creating it a Month-3-8 deliverable, not a Month-12 afterthought.

On the AMBITIOUS variant Matt asked about — the honest GTM read: the consulting business is correctly sized for SOLO, but the constraint that caps it is delivery capacity, not demand or positioning. The ambitious move that survives every v5 critique is NOT funding/SaaS (the general engine is correctly rejected, and the spin-out buyer is a different developer persona). It is a PRODUCTIZED-SERVICE-WITH-LEVERAGED-DELIVERY play: once 3-4 First Closes in a COMMON stack (e.g., QuickBooks + Bill.com + Gusto) prove the pattern library cuts hand-assembly time, hire/contract ONE junior finance-ops associate to do the mapping working-sessions under Matt's review. That breaks the ~40-week ceiling along the only axis that's actually binding (Matt's hours on repeatable mapping work) without betting on a general engine, without changing the liability posture (the buyer's reviewer still signs), and without the runway math collapsing (the associate is funded by Year-2 anchor margin, not speculative capital). This is the ambitious variant that is still HONEST: it scales delivery capacity, not a software fantasy. v5's ceiling is real for a pure soloist but is a CHOICE, not a law — and the document should name the associate-leverage path as the sanctioned Phase-2 growth lever, demoting the developer-facing spin-out to the genuine long-shot it is.

Add a named 'losing trigger' to the GTM plan: the single fastest way to know the positioning is wrong is if warm finance-operator intros consistently respond 'our controller already handles this fine.' That response means the capacity-strain trigger isn't acute enough in the contacted segment — and the fix is to tighten the ICP toward companies that JUST added a system or JUST got a tighter board/lender cadence (the genuinely acute sub-triggers in Section 3a), not to lower the price. Instrument the first 10 conversations for which sub-trigger was present; let that data re-aim the ICP rather than guessing.

strengths: The liability posture is now genuinely honest and verified-correct. The single-signer rule (Section 1, Risk #2) — the buyer's own in-house reviewer signs, never Matt, never a supplied CPA — is the right E&O answer, and it is structurally enforced by the buyer-selection rule (no-reviewer firms are not served). I verified Matt is a financial analyst not a CPA (memory/user_role.md, controller.md grants no credential), so positioning him as drafter-not-signer is the only defensible stance and v5 holds it consistently. The fractional-CPA-partner trap that sank v3 is gone.

v5 is unusually honest about the governance assets being real-but-unwired. I confirmed all of it on disk: guard-destructive.sh / guard-web-access.sh / guard-recursive-spawn.sh exist and fire PreToolUse; thing-decide.py produces real verdicts; the 5 Saga records under .ravenclaude/runs/thing/decisions/ are all marketplace-development decisions, never finance. Risk #4 states this plainly ('the governance evidence is currently about the wrong subject') and the 4b status table marks the gated-AI-verdict-under-a-number as Aspirational. That is the correct, non-embarrassing framing.

The deterministic-script-the-reviewer-re-runs design (Section 4c) is the right assurance architecture for the existential numbers-liability risk. Numbers come from a fixed, reviewed, reproducible script the buyer re-runs — not LLM free-text. That is a real, auditor-defensible control story (reproducibility + human sign-off), and v5 correctly makes it structural rather than a disclaimer.

The 'Evidence Pack,' not 'Certificate' rename (Risk #7) is the correct assurance-honesty move — no accredited body stands behind it, so 'evidence you can show' avoids implying certification. The insurance/E&O trigger is correctly demoted to unverified-secondary (Risk #8, Section 3), so the demand thesis no longer rests on aggregator statistics that an analytical buyer would catch.

gaps.title: No data-handling, confidentiality, retention, or breach posture anywhere in the concept — a finance consultant holding customers' GL exports is a breach-and-confidentiality exposure that is currently undefined
gaps.why: I grepped the full v5 doc for retention/breach/confidential/PII/encrypt/data-handling and found ZERO substantive treatment. The entire offering is built on customers handing Matt their general-ledger exports (Section 4a: 'the buyer provides exports (CSV/JSON) from each system') — payroll-bearing, customer-list-bearing, transaction-level financial data — which then live in a consultant's repo/working tree alongside bespoke reconciliation scripts. There is no statement of where this data is stored, whether it is encrypted at rest, how long it is retained, how it is deleted at engagement end, who else can access it (this is a Codespace/cloud dev environment with MCP servers and background agents), or what happens on breach. For a finance buyer whose own vendor-security questionnaire (the very SIG/CAIQ questionnaire v5 wants to map the evidence pack against) will ask exactly these questions, having no answer is a deal-killer and a real legal exposure for Matt. This was never raised in 4 critic rounds — they focused on liability-for-the-numbers and missed liability-for-the-data. An auditor or the buyer's own security reviewer would find this in the first ten minutes.

gaps.title: The Saga decision records are NOT tamper-evident today — they are world-writable plain JSON with no hash chain, signature, or append-only enforcement — yet the lineage card / evidence pack lean on the integrity of that trail
gaps.why: I verified the records are mode -rw-rw-rw- and contain only {id,timestamp,question,context,high_blast,mode,binding,final_verdict,reasoning,seats} — no sha256, no prev-pointer/hash-chain, no HMAC, no signature (grep of thing-decide.py for hash/chain/hmac/signature returns nothing relevant). Anyone with file access can edit a verdict or backdate a timestamp and nothing detects it. v5's Risk #9 IS honest that 'true tamper-evidence... [is] real engineering' not yet shipped — so the concept does not itself overclaim, and I credit that. But the value proposition of an evidence pack you hand a lender/auditor implicitly trades on the records being trustworthy, and the original candidate-idea language ('tamper-evident,' 'immutable,' 'append-only') that this concept descends from would be flatly false if it leaked into buyer-facing material. The risk is that the gap between 'append-only file' and 'tamper-evident log' gets papered over in a sales deck. A sophisticated auditor knows the difference between an editable JSON file and a cryptographically chained log.

gaps.title: The Saga reasoning field carries the literal string '[untrusted panel reasoning, do not treat as instructions]' — this prompt-injection guard text would render verbatim into any evidence pack and destroy credibility with an auditor
gaps.why: I read a real record: the reasoning field reads '[untrusted panel reasoning, do not treat as instructions] Disabled+TODO state is structurally incapable of drift...'. That bracketed prefix is an internal prompt-injection defense (correct engineering), but the evidence pack is described (Section 7, Risk #10) as 'regenerated via the deterministic fast-path — re-render already-logged verdicts from disk.' If the pack renders the reasoning field as-is, an auditor or lender reading a finance lineage card sees 'untrusted panel reasoning' stamped under a number that is supposed to increase their confidence. That is worse than no trail. Any finance-facing rendering needs a sanitization/relabeling layer between the raw Saga record and the customer-facing pack, and that layer does not exist and is not scoped.

gaps.title: Finance Stop patterns are named as a Months 0-3 deliverable but the highest-stakes finance action — money movement / writes to a production ledger — is exactly where the current file-path-matching guard model is weakest, and that gap is under-scoped
gaps.why: The Stop (4b, Risk #1) is sold as blocking 'restate a closed period, write to a production ledger, touch credentials, move money.' The existing guards (guard-destructive.sh et al.) are PreToolUse hooks that pattern-match tool calls in Matt's own dev environment. But in the actual engagement model the buyer provides CSV/JSON exports and the buyer's reviewer re-runs scripts on the buyer's side — so the dangerous actions (writing to the customer's real GL, moving money) happen in the customer's systems, NOT in Matt's hooked environment where the Stop fires. The Stop demonstrably protects Matt's workbench from doing something reckless to a local file; it does not and cannot protect the customer's production ledger, because Matt's tooling never touches it. v5 should be explicit that the Stop is a control over the workbench's own actions on the provided exports, not a control over the customer's live financial systems — otherwise the demo ('watch it block restating a locked close') implies a protection scope the architecture doesn't have.

closures: Add a Section (e.g. 4d 'Data handling & confidentiality') and a corresponding Risk that states the concrete posture: (1) customer GL exports are scoped to per-engagement isolated storage (a dedicated encrypted-at-rest volume or a separate repo per client, NOT the shared RavenClaude working tree); (2) a stated retention window with mandatory deletion at engagement close + a deletion attestation the buyer receives; (3) a one-page DPA/confidentiality + data-handling addendum in the engagement contract; (4) an explicit statement that the build environment (Codespace, MCP servers, background agents) is hardened so customer financial data is not exposed to third-party tool calls or web egress (the guard-web-access.sh egress backstop is the existing asset to point at). This also becomes a SALES ASSET: it pre-answers the buyer's own vendor-security questionnaire, turning a gap into a differentiator.

Pin the integrity claim precisely: in Risk #9 and anywhere the evidence pack is described, state that today the decision log is 'append-only-by-convention, not cryptographically tamper-evident,' and that the buyer-facing pack is sold as 'a contemporaneous, dated record,' never 'tamper-evident' or 'immutable,' until a hash-chain/signature layer ships. Add a small, real, near-term hardening to the roadmap (hash-chain each record to its predecessor + a signed daily digest) so the claim can graduate honestly — this is a few days of work, not the 'multi-tenant SaaS' the doc lumps it with, so it should be separated out and scheduled.

Add a customer-facing rendering/sanitization layer to the evidence-pack milestone (Section 8, Months 8-12) that maps raw Saga fields to auditor-safe labels and STRIPS the '[untrusted panel reasoning...]' injection-guard prefix (and any other internal markers) before render. Treat the prefix as an internal-only field; the pack should show a relabeled 'panel rationale (advisory)' with the bracket text removed. Add a fixture test that fails if the injection-guard string ever appears in rendered customer output.

Reword the Stop scope in 4b, Section 6, and Risk #1 to be precise: the Stop is a control over the workbench's own actions on the provided exports and on locally-staged files, not a control over the customer's live ERP/GL/banking systems (which the tooling never connects to). Reframe the demo line accordingly: 'watch the workbench refuse to overwrite the locked-period working file' rather than implying it guards the customer's production ledger. This keeps the trust-close honest and pre-empts the skeptical controller's 'but it can't actually touch my real GL, so what is it stopping?' objection.
enhancements: Turn the data-handling posture into the wedge's sharpest competitive edge against in-tool AI (Section 3b). Excel Copilot / accounting-suite native AI run inside the customer's own tenant on live data — a higher data-exposure surface than they may realize. A consultant who works ONLY from scoped, deletable exports and hands back a signed deletion attestation can credibly say 'your most sensitive financial data never touches a third-party AI's training-eligible context, and we prove its deletion' — which is exactly the assurance story a security-conscious controller wants, and it is a story the incumbents structurally cannot tell. This converts the P0 gap into a differentiator.

Ship a minimal, honest 'integrity ledger' as the first finance-governance feature: hash-chain each decision record to its predecessor and publish a daily signed digest. This is small (days, not the SaaS-scale engineering the doc fears), it makes 'tamper-evident' true-and-sayable, and it is the single most auditor-impressive upgrade to the evidence pack — it converts the P1 integrity gap into a demonstrable control with near-zero ongoing cost.

For the AMBITIOUS variant the brief asks about: the honest blocker to ANY team/funded/SaaS scaling is the two unshipped assurance layers — multi-tenant data isolation and tamper-evident logs. So the correct ambitious sequencing is to fund those two as a deliberate, named 'assurance hardening' track inside the consulting cash flow (not a speculative SaaS sprint). Concretely: the spin-out fork (Section 7) should be gated not only on '3+ stable engagements' but on 'multi-tenant isolation + tamper-evident logging shipped and independently pen-tested.' That makes the ambitious path survivable rather than aspirational — a partner or funding becomes justifiable precisely when those two assurance properties are real, because they are the only things standing between the solo service and a defensible product, and they are also the things a solo founder is least equipped to harden alone. That is the ambition-without-over-correction answer: stay solo-service until the assurance substrate is real, then the funded/team variant has a genuine moat to scale rather than a dogfooded single-repo prototype.

strengths: The Year-1-as-funded-runway reframe (Section 7/8) is the single most important fix in v5 and it is executed correctly from a PM standpoint: it stops booking the build year as an income year, moves the $160-200k floor to Year 2, and explicitly names the retained-partial-income / savings draw as a precondition rather than discovering the shortfall later. This is exactly the reconciliation the round-4 critic demanded (major weakness #2), and v5 does not paper over it.

The 'build the engine inside paid delivery' sequencing (Months 3-8 design-partner phase) is the right de-risking move: it eliminates the unfunded 4-6 month solo engine sprint, puts the hardest technical work (reconciliation) on real customer books where it has to survive, and funds it as R&D + customer acquisition. The dependency chain (demo -> design partners -> patterns harvested via /wrap -> full-price) is correctly ordered.

The bespoke-not-general reconciliation reframe (Section 4c) removes the most unexecutable line item in v4 (a 4-month solo BlackLine). Per-customer hand-built scripts that the buyer's reviewer re-runs is a plan a solo operator can actually deliver week-by-week, and it correctly relocates the cross-system-identity problem from 'auto-solve at runtime' (impossible) to 'human decides the join rules once per customer' (a billable working-session).

The fork-gate honesty (Section 8) is strong PM discipline: v5 concedes the stability sample may be under-powered at month 18, sets the hard rule 'do not build multi-tenant SaaS until >=3 full-price engagements close AND the pattern library demonstrably cut build hours,' and makes 'stay consulting' the safe default. This converts the spin-out from a load-bearing plan assumption into gated optionality, which is where it belongs.

The instruction to instrument delivery hours from day one (Months 12-18) to MEASURE rather than assert the compounding claim is the correct response to a recurring critic theme across rounds. It turns the central efficiency thesis into a falsifiable metric.

gaps.title: The Year-1 runway precondition is named but never quantified or time-boxed - 'partial analyst income or a defined savings draw' has no hours-arithmetic, so its feasibility (the explicit question in my brief) is asserted, not shown.
gaps.why: Section 7 says Matt keeps 'partial analyst income OR draws a defined savings runway' while delivering 1-2 design partners in Months 0-8. But a 4-6 week design-partner build that ALSO involves hand-building bespoke reconciliation scripts on messy first-customer books (the hardest, slowest version of the work, before any pattern library exists) is plausibly MORE than 4-6 weeks of effort, not less. Two such engagements overlapping Months 3-8 could consume 12-16 effort-weeks of a ~26-week half-year. There is no calculation showing how many hours/week of analyst income Matt can hold while delivering that, what the savings-draw figure actually is, or what the runway floor is below which the plan does not start. Risk #5 names 'if Matt has no runway, the plan doesn't start' but never sizes the runway. A precondition you cannot evaluate is a plan you cannot commit to. This is the same class of error (math computed in the abstract, never reconciled against the week-by-week schedule) that the round-4 critic caught in v4's floor math - v5 fixed it for Year 2 but left the Year-1 runway side unquantified.

gaps.title: Single-customer dependency in the design-partner phase is an unaddressed concentration risk that can sink the whole build, not just a deal.
gaps.why: The entire pattern library - v5's only compounding asset and the thing that makes Year-2 margins work - is built inside 1-2 design-partner engagements (Section 8, Months 3-8). If ONE of two design partners churns mid-engagement, pauses, has a data-access problem, or turns out to have an idiosyncratic stack that yields no reusable patterns, the R&D foundation for the whole business is halved or lost, AND the subsidized revenue that funds the runway evaporates. Risk #6 covers first-deal SOURCING concentration (one channel) but not design-partner DELIVERY concentration (the asset-building phase rests on 1-2 specific accounts completing successfully). With n=1-2 there is no redundancy on the single most load-bearing phase of the plan.

gaps.title: Platform/vendor risk is entirely absent from the risk register, despite the whole delivery stack riding on Anthropic (Claude API pricing, model availability, claude -p for the tribunal, plugin mechanics).
gaps.why: Every deliverable - the agent-assisted script-building, the demo, the tribunal seats (claude -p), the lineage card, the governance leash - depends on Claude/Anthropic. The original Forge candidate idea explicitly listed 'Anthropic/platform dependency: a breaking change to the plugin/marketplace model or a pricing shift could force a costly re-platform' as an honest risk, and the Verdict/Leash candidates flagged per-decision token cost and 'Anthropic ships a native governance panel' - yet v5's 11-item risk register names NONE of these as a standalone platform risk. A 2-3x Claude API price increase changes the cost-truth math in Section 7 (the tribunal and agent-assisted build both consume tokens); a plugin-mechanics breaking change forces re-platform; an Anthropic-native cross-system + audit-trail feature is the same encroachment v5 only credits to Microsoft (Risk #11). For a one-person business whose entire production capability is one vendor, omitting this is a material risk-register gap.

gaps.title: Key-person / health / continuity risk is unaddressed, and for a model whose moat is explicitly 'Matt himself' (Section 5) this is the highest-consequence single point of failure for the buyer, not just the founder.
gaps.why: Section 5 honestly states two-thirds of the moat IS Matt (senior judgment + founder credibility, both single-person dependencies). The run-it retainer (Section 7, $5-9k/mo) operates a SIGNED monthly close on a hard calendar - if Matt is unavailable (illness, injury, burnout, a family event - he has a family per memory), the customer's close does not happen that month, which for a covenant-monitored or board-reporting buyer is a real harm and a reputational/contractual exposure. v5 names solo capacity as 'the binding constraint AND the ceiling' (Risk #5) but treats it purely as a throughput cap, never as a continuity/bus-factor risk to the customer's recurring deliverable. There is no described fallback (a vetted backup contractor, a documented runbook the buyer's reviewer could execute, a force-majeure clause).

gaps.title: The Months 0-3 deliverables over-pack the critical path and the 'stretch' tribunal-wiring item is mis-scoped as optional when the demo's trust-close depends on a related capability.
gaps.why: Months 0-3 must simultaneously: build the finance-value-first demo on a sample multi-system GL set, define finance Stop patterns, AND source 1-2 design partners from a cold-ish network - while Matt is on partial income (so part-time). Sourcing alone (a skeptical, conservative finance buyer per the candidate-idea risks, long sales cycle) can consume the whole window. The demo build (a working lineage card over a reconciled sample, plus a live Stop) is itself non-trivial for a non-developer-founder even with agent help. Bundling sourcing + demo-build + Stop-pattern-definition into one part-time 3-month window repeats the over-packed-milestone pattern the round-2 critic flagged (Month 0-1 bundling three hard things). The plan would benefit from explicitly making sourcing the long-pole that runs in parallel across all of Months 0-8, not a Months 0-3 task.

closures: Add a one-paragraph Year-1 runway arithmetic to Section 7: (a) state the assumed effort-weeks per design-partner build at the HARD end (e.g. 6-8 weeks each because patterns don't exist yet and first-customer books are messiest), (b) net that against a stated part-time analyst commitment (e.g. 'Matt holds ~20 hrs/wk of analyst income, leaving ~20 hrs/wk for delivery' or whatever is real), (c) compute the resulting savings-draw needed to cover the gap between ~$50-90k Year-1 consulting margin and the household burn, and (d) state the explicit GO/NO-GO precondition: 'Do not start unless [N] months of runway are confirmed.' Even rough numbers convert an un-evaluable precondition into a checkable gate.

Add a design-partner-delivery concentration mitigation to Risk #6 (or a new sub-risk): aim for 2 design partners specifically chosen for COMMON, contrasting stacks (e.g. QuickBooks+Bill.com+Gusto vs Xero+Stripe) so the pattern library has redundancy and breadth from the start; and add a contingency clause that if one design partner stalls past a defined checkpoint (e.g. mapping not completable in the first working-session), Matt sources a replacement before continuing to full-price, rather than proceeding on n=1.

Add a 'Platform / vendor-dependency risk' item to Section 9: name the three concrete exposures (Claude API pricing shift changing the Section-7 cost-truth and per-decision tribunal economics; Claude Code plugin/marketplace breaking change forcing re-platform of the delivery substrate; Anthropic shipping a native cross-system/audit-trail feature). Mitigation: keep the deterministic reconciliation scripts model-agnostic (they are hand-built Python/SQL, not LLM-dependent at runtime - state this explicitly as the hedge), price engagements with margin headroom for a token-cost increase, and treat the leash/governance layer as the differentiator least replaceable by a single-vendor feature.

Add a key-person / continuity item to Section 9: for the run-it retainer specifically, require (a) a written runbook per customer that THEIR reviewer can follow to execute the close from the bespoke scripts without Matt, (b) a force-majeure / availability clause in the retainer contract capping Matt's continuity obligation, and (c) a stated intent to identify a vetted backup finance-analyst contractor before the retainer base grows past ~2 accounts. This also doubles as the first honest answer to 'what scales past Matt' - a bench, not software.

Restructure Section 8's timeline so sourcing is a continuous long-pole spanning Months 0-8 (run in parallel), and move the 'stretch' tribunal-wiring item out of the Months 0-3 critical path entirely into Months 8-12 where it is honestly an enhancement, not a demo dependency. Keep Months 0-3 to two committed deliverables (demo + Stop patterns) with sourcing explicitly flagged as the gating risk for the whole phase.
enhancements: Add an explicit pipeline-coverage ratio to the plan: a conservative finance buyer with a long sales cycle means landing 1-2 design partners likely requires 8-15 qualified conversations. State the target top-of-funnel count and where it comes from (Matt's network + the named fractional-CFO referral channel from Risk #6), so 'source 1-2 design partners' becomes a measurable funnel, not a hope. This directly de-risks the P0 runway gap because sourcing slippage is what extends the unpaid window.

Define one or two leading indicators that distinguish 'the runway is working' from 'I am quietly going broke' by month 6 - e.g. first design-partner signed by month 3, first working close delivered by month 5, build-hours-per-deliverable trending down by the second engagement. Pair each with a documented pivot/stop action. This gives Matt an early abort signal instead of discovering the shortfall at month 12.

On the AMBITIOUS variant the brief asks about: the cleanest ambition lever that survives v5's critiques is NOT a SaaS spin-out (correctly gated and low-probability) and NOT a general engine (correctly rejected) - it is a deliberate, funded second hire: a junior finance-analyst contractor brought in during Year 2, trained on the now-mature pattern library, to absorb the lower-judgment reconciliation-script assembly and the run-it retainers. That lifts the ~40-delivery-week ceiling without betting the business on unbuildable software, doubles as the key-person/continuity mitigation, and converts 'the moat is Matt' into 'the moat is Matt's documented method + a small bench.' This is the ambition-honoring path that survives the liability posture (the buyer's reviewer still signs), the no-general-engine constraint (the bench builds bespoke scripts faster, not a general product), and the runway math (funded from Year-2 margin, not speculative capital). Worth adding as a named, gated Year-2+ fork ALONGSIDE the spin-out option, since it is higher-probability and lower-risk than the SaaS bet the document currently treats as the sole scale lever.

Consider sequencing the FIRST design partner as a stack Matt ALREADY knows intimately from his own analyst work, to maximize the odds the foundational engagement succeeds and yields clean patterns - treat design-partner #1 as the lowest-risk possible delivery, not the highest-revenue, since its job is to build the asset, not the income.
